title: Supplement/herb + unproven-diet caution content (evidence-based, defer-to-team)
context: Patients in cancer treatment frequently encounter online claims about supplements, herbs, and 'anti-cancer diets' (alkaline, ketogenic-as-therapy, juicing, extreme fasting, 'starve the tumor' claims). The project must address these directly and accurately without endorsing them or providing dosing/interaction advice. This caution content is HIGH risk because inaccurate or alarming framing can cause patients to stop eating adequately, abandon effective treatment, or take harmful doses. Depends on nutrition-during-treatment-sources-007 and nutrition-during-treatment-content-008. Content is education only — no dietary prescriptions or endorsements.
objective: Author supplement/herb and unproven-diet caution content that accurately states, with citations, what authoritative bodies and evidence say about common supplements/herbs and unproven 'anti-cancer' diets. The content must not endorse, prescribe doses, or judge drug-herb interactions for individuals. It must emphasize eating enough, use a non-alarming tone, and route all individual supplement and diet questions to the pharmacist, dietitian, and care team.
acceptanceCriteria: Content addresses common supplements/herbs (e.g., antioxidants, turmeric/curcumin, cannabis/CBD, mushroom supplements, high-dose vitamins) and unproven diets (alkaline, ketogenic-as-therapy, juicing/detox, extreme fasting, 'starve the tumor') by citing what authoritative bodies and published evidence actually show — not by endorsing or detailing the claims

Content explicitly does not endorse any supplement or unproven diet as anti-cancer or curative; does not recommend doses or brands; does not judge drug-herb or drug-nutrient interactions for individuals; all such individual questions are routed to the pharmacist, oncology dietitian, and care team
Tone is non-alarming and constructive: emphasizes that eating enough during treatment is a high priority; frames caution content as information to discuss with the care team, not as prohibition

Every claim cited to a license-verified authoritative source (oncology guidelines, NCI/PDQ, peer-reviewed evidence); citation-coverage CI gate passes; content passes grade 8 or below readability check

Not-medical-advice banner present; red-flag escalation block included where appropriate (e.g., for supplement interactions that may affect treatment efficacy); banner-lint and escalation-lint CI gates pass

Credentialed oncology RDN/CSO sign-off recorded; oncologist sign-off recorded for clinically-loaded claims (e.g., supplement-treatment interaction cautions); patient-advocate sign-off for tone and readability recorded
output: Plain-language, evidence-cited, expert-signed-off caution content on supplements/herbs and unproven anti-cancer diets, written in a non-alarming defer-to-team style, merged into the content corpus and passing all CI gates.
